summary: Gruber argues that major PC makers like Dell, Sony, and HP should stop relying on Microsoft Windows and create their own operating systems. He responds to critics of his earlier 'Herd Mentality' piece who warned that multiple desktop OSes would create compatibility nightmares, countering that the web has fundamentally changed the interoperability landscape since the early PC era. He points to the Litl webbook and Palm's WebOS as proof that even small companies can build novel operating systems, making the inaction of well-resourced PC makers inexcusable. His core argument is that these companies lack not resources but ambition and passion for great software.
key_points: Software matters more than hardware — Gruber would prefer a PC running Mac OS X to a Mac running Windows
The web has eliminated the interoperability problems that made DOS/Windows dominance inevitable in the 1980s
The DOS/Windows monopoly feedback loop was 'sticky' in ways that today's platforms are not, because the web is now the universal platform
Small companies like Litl and Palm prove that building a novel OS is achievable, making big PC makers' inaction a choice, not a constraint
Major PC makers are dying not from lack of resources or opportunity but from lack of ambition and passion for great software
money_quotes: It should be embarrassing to companies like Dell and Sony, with deep pockets and strong brand names, that they're stuck selling computers with the same copy of Windows installed as the no-name brands.
All things considered I'd much prefer a PC running Mac OS X to a Mac running Windows.
It may have been a mess, but it was a beautiful mess. It was glorious. It was fun.
The DOS/Windows monopoly grew impregnable because it was a platform where the only way to play along was to join it.
The Web is a bigger platform than Windows. The Web is universal. Every computer is on the Web.
Hey, maybe computers in 2010 can do better than a user experience that is fundamentally unchanged from the original Macintosh in 1984.
Has HP made anything this decade that anyone will remember fondly even five years from now? Inkjet printers?
These PC makers are lacking in neither financial resources nor opportunity. What they're lacking is ambition, gumption, and passion for great software and new frontiers. They're busy dying.
key_insight: The web has destroyed the interoperability moat that made Windows dominance inevitable, leaving major PC makers with no excuse for their failure to build differentiated operating systems except a fatal lack of ambition.
